name: Code Archaeology and Change Tracking with Git Blame
description: Use git blame and git log to trace the history of code changes, understand why lines were written, and find the context behind decisions.
prerequisites: A repository with meaningful commit history

steps.title: Blame a file to see line-by-line authorship
steps.description: Show each line of a file annotated with the commit hash, author, and date of the last change. This tells you who changed each line and when.
steps.tip: Use 'git blame -w' to ignore whitespace changes, which often clutter blame output after reformatting.

steps.title: Blame a specific line range
steps.description: Focus on lines 45 through 65. This is much faster than blaming the entire file when you know which lines you are investigating.

steps.title: See the commit details for a blamed line
steps.description: Once blame identifies the commit hash, use git show to see the full commit diff, message, and context of why the change was made.

steps.title: Trace a line through renames and moves
steps.description: Search for commits that added or removed the string 'validateToken' in the file, even across renames. The -p flag shows the actual diff.
steps.tip: Use -G instead of -S for regex pattern matching. -S finds commits where the count of the string changes, while -G finds commits where the diff matches the pattern.

steps.title: Ignore bulk formatting commits in blame
steps.description: Skip a specific commit (like a code formatting change) in blame output. Git will attribute the lines to the previous meaningful change instead.
steps.tip: Create a .git-blame-ignore-revs file with commit hashes to always ignore, then configure it with: git config blame.ignoreRevsFile .git-blame-ignore-revs

steps.title: View file history with full diffs
steps.description: Show the complete change history of a file with diffs for each commit. The --follow flag tracks the file even if it was renamed.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# git-archaeology.sh — Code archaeology helper
set -euo pipefail

FILE="${1:?Usage: $0 <file> [line-start] [line-end] [search-string]}"
LINE_START="${2:-}"
LINE_END="${3:-}"
SEARCH="${4:-}"

echo "=== File: $FILE ==="
echo ""

if [ -n "$SEARCH" ]; then
  echo "--- Commits containing '$SEARCH' ---"
  git log --oneline -S "$SEARCH" --follow -- "$FILE"
  echo ""
  echo "--- Diffs for '$SEARCH' ---"
  git log -p -S "$SEARCH" --follow -- "$FILE" | head -100
elif [ -n "$LINE_START" ] && [ -n "$LINE_END" ]; then
  echo "--- Blame for lines $LINE_START-$LINE_END ---"
  git blame -w -L "$LINE_START,$LINE_END" "$FILE"
  echo ""
  echo "--- Log for lines $LINE_START-$LINE_END ---"
  git log --oneline -L "$LINE_START,$LINE_END:$FILE" | head -40
else
  echo "--- Blame (ignoring whitespace) ---"
  git blame -w "$FILE" | head -50
  echo ""
  if [ "$(git blame "$FILE" | wc -l)" -gt 50 ]; then
    echo "(showing first 50 lines, use line range for specifics)"
  fi
  echo ""
  echo "--- Recent file history ---"
  git log --oneline --follow -10 -- "$FILE"
fi

faq.question: How do I ignore a Prettier or formatting commit in blame?
faq.answer: Create a file called .git-blame-ignore-revs in your repository root, add the formatting commit hash on its own line, and run 'git config blame.ignoreRevsFile .git-blame-ignore-revs'. Commit this file so all team members benefit. GitHub also supports this file automatically.

faq.question: What is the difference between git blame and git log for tracking changes?
faq.answer: Git blame shows the last commit that modified each line of a file at a specific point in time. Git log shows the full sequence of commits that changed a file over time. Use blame to find who last changed a line, and log to see the full evolution of a file.

faq.question: Can git blame show the original author if a line was moved from another file?
faq.answer: Use 'git blame -C' to detect lines that were copied or moved from other files in the same commit. Use '-C -C' (twice) to also search in the commit that created the file, and '-C -C -C' to search all commits. This significantly increases processing time.
